title: Akamai CDN
subtitle: A copy of your content in every neighbourhood

steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: A CDN is like having a local convenience store on every street corner. Customers don't walk to the central warehouse — they grab the closest copy of what they need.

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
Akamai CDN caches content at 4,000+ PoPs globally. Configuration lives in Property Manager — a hierarchical rule tree. Common products:

- Ion: full-site acceleration with mPulse RUM bundled
- AMD (Adaptive Media Delivery): live + on-demand video, ABR
- DSA (Dynamic Site Accelerator): TCP optimization for non-cacheable APIs

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: Activate a Property to staging first, validate, then promote to production. Use Fast Purge for invalidation. Origin is usually Object Storage (static), a NodeBalancer (dynamic), or both via origin selection rules.
"CDN = your content closer to users. Property Manager = the rule tree that controls everything. Stage first, always."

quiz.question: You're shipping a new Property change. Correct order?

options.text: Activate to staging → validate → promote to production
options.explain: Property Manager has separate staging and production networks. Stage first, validate, then promote.

options.text: Edit and refresh — Property Manager auto-applies
options.explain: No auto-apply. Activations are explicit and versioned.
Fast Purge is for cache invalidation, not deployment.

quiz.question: Your origin is Object Storage. You also need to fall back to a Linode for dynamic API calls. How?

options.explain: A single Property can route to multiple origins.

options.text: Use origin selection rules — match path or host, route to the right origin
options.explain: Property Manager rules can select different origins per path/host. Static → Object Storage. /api → Linode/NodeBalancer.
You'd lose CDN intelligence and per-path routing.
